channel.title: The Daily Healthcare
channel.description: A 31-episode walk through the U.S. healthcare system for investors, PMs, analysts, and operators. Each episode unpacks the mechanics, money anchors, and tensions behind how care actually gets paid for — from payer economics and Medicare Advantage to RCM, drug pricing, and value-based care.
channel.itunes:author: Reggie Yang
channel.itunes:summary: A 31-episode walk through the U.S. healthcare system for investors, PMs, analysts, and operators — the mechanics, money anchors, and tensions behind how care gets paid for.

item.title: Episode 1: Why America pays double for worse healthcare
item.description: The U.S. spends ~$15,500 per capita on healthcare — more than double the OECD average — and gets worse life expectancy. We walk through the four design choices behind the gap: why healthcare breaks normal market rules, the five players (and the fully-insured vs self-insured split most people miss), the ten-step revenue cycle, and the WWII-era path dependence the U.S. can't easily unwind.
item.itunes:summary: Why the U.S. pays double for worse outcomes — the four design choices behind a $5.3T, 18%-of-GDP system.

item.title: Episode 2: The insurance card lie most Americans believe
item.description: Two workers at the same employer with same-looking insurance cards can be on structurally different products — and most never know it. We unpack insurance as risk-pooling, the fully-insured vs self-insured split (~6 in 10 commercially covered Americans are on self-insured plans), the HMO/PPO network tradeoff, and Medicare Parts A and B — including Part B's unlimited 20% exposure that created the Medigap shadow industry.
item.itunes:summary: For most Americans with employer coverage, the "insurance company" is just an administrator — the employer is the real payer.

item.title: Episode 3: How Medicare Advantage flipped the whole game
item.description: Medicare Advantage is the fastest-growing health insurance category in America — and the business model is almost the opposite of what most people assume "Medicare" means. We cover private plans paid a monthly check per member, Part D pharmacy, Medicaid's dual role as safety net and long-term care payer, and the government programs (VA, TRICARE, IHS) that sit outside the main system.
item.itunes:summary: Medicare Advantage's capitated business model — and why it flipped the economics of insuring seniors.

item.title: Episode 4: The real hospital price no one sees
item.description: A doctor's visit can fail before the patient walks in the door — and the bill lands two months later. Front-end eligibility and back-end contracting are the invisible plumbing that decide whether care gets paid for. We cover the ACA marketplace, eligibility mechanics, payer-provider contracting, and why the "real" price of a hospital procedure is buried inside negotiated rates that almost no one ever sees.
item.itunes:summary: Marketplace, eligibility, and contracting — the invisible plumbing that decides whether care gets paid for.

item.title: Episode 5: The math rule that built UnitedHealth's empire
item.description: A health insurer that runs "too efficient" is legally required to mail refund checks to its members. That one arithmetic constraint — the Medical Loss Ratio — quietly explains UnitedHealth's Optum strategy. We walk through self-insured plans, point solutions, payer economics, and why the MLR floor reshaped the vertical integration playbook for every major payer.
item.itunes:summary: The Medical Loss Ratio — the one arithmetic rule behind Optum and the modern payer vertical integration playbook.

item.title: Episode 6: Why the same procedure costs $3,500 or $700
item.description: The same colonoscopy, same doctor, same equipment can cost $3,500 or $700 depending on which room the billing code says it happened in. That single fact explains a decade of hospital M&A. We cover the provider landscape — hospitals, ASCs, physician practices — and the patient journey through the site-of-service arbitrage that's quietly reshaping where care actually happens.
item.itunes:summary: Site-of-service arbitrage — why the same procedure costs wildly different amounts, and what that did to hospital M&A.

item.title: Episode 7: Why doctors spend two hours charting per patient hour
item.description: The number one cause of physician burnout isn't patients or night shifts — it's the chart. For every hour with a patient, doctors spend roughly two hours documenting. We unpack documentation burden, the workforce crisis it's driving, post-acute care dynamics, and why the administrative tax on clinical time has become healthcare's most pressing operational problem.
item.itunes:summary: The documentation tax — 2 hours of charting per patient hour, and the workforce crisis it's creating.

item.title: Episode 8: Why Medicare won't pay for grandma's nursing home
item.description: Medicare does not pay for a nursing home. If Grandma needs long-term custodial care, the system's plan is: spend every dollar she has until she's poor enough for Medicaid. That's not a gap — it's the design. We cover long-term care financing, behavioral health's payment maze, digital health economics, and the consolidation wave reshaping who owns what.
item.itunes:summary: LTC, behavioral health, digital health, and consolidation — the spend-down design most families never see coming.

item.title: Episode 9: How healthcare giants hide their wealth
item.description: A hospital can report a 3% operating margin and be either in great shape or near default. The difference is in footnotes that don't make the press release. Healthcare financial statements have their own grammar — we teach you how to read them, and how to spot what M&A activity is really signaling about payer, provider, and pharma balance sheets.
item.itunes:summary: How to read healthcare financial statements — and what M&A activity actually signals.

item.title: Episode 10: The $300B paperwork industry hiding in your bill
item.description: The U.S. spends roughly $250–300 billion a year just on the paperwork of billing for healthcare — more than it spends on all cancer drugs combined. That number is why RCM is a $100B+ industry. We cover the revenue cycle end-to-end: patient access, eligibility, and the coding basics that determine whether a claim ever gets paid.
item.itunes:summary: $250–300B/year spent just on billing paperwork — why RCM is a $100B+ industry.
